content.text: Modern LLM applications are increasingly governed not by model weights but by the instructions and examples passed in the prompt. DSPy — the Declarative Self-improving Python framework — treats prompt content as an optimizable program parameter: instead of writing instructions and few-shot examples by hand, a compiler searches the space of candidate prompts using a training set and a metric function, then writes the best prompt it finds directly into the program. The resulting system performs better on the measured objective, adapts automatically when the task changes, and requires far less manual trial-and-error than traditional prompt engineering.

Automatic prompt optimization (APO) is the practice of using algorithms — rather than human intuition — to search for, evaluate, and select the best prompt for a given task and model. DSPy, introduced by Stanford NLP in 2023, is the most widely adopted open-source framework for this approach. In DSPy, developers write programs that describe

) then uses a training set and a metric function to automatically synthesize the actual prompt text — instructions and few-shot demonstrations — that maximizes the metric. The compiled program contains "baked-in" prompts that were discovered algorithmically, not authored by hand.

DSPy separates three concerns that are typically conflated in manual prompt engineering: program logic (Python code defining the pipeline of LLM calls), task specification (Signatures declaring field types and semantics), and optimization (which optimizer and metric to use). This separation means the same program can be recompiled for a different model or a different data distribution simply by changing the optimizer configuration — no prompt rewriting required.

The framework ships several optimizers — BootstrapFewShot, BootstrapFewShotWithRandomSearch, MIPROv2, and COPRO — each implementing a different search strategy over prompt space. All of them share the same outer interface: call
and receive back a compiled program with optimized prompts embedded.

Manual prompt engineering has three compounding problems. First, it is slow: a skilled engineer might spend days iterating on a prompt before converging on something acceptable. Second, it is brittle: a prompt tuned on a handful of handpicked examples often degrades on the real distribution of inputs that appear in production. Third, it does not transfer: when the underlying model is upgraded, or when the task requirements shift, the entire effort must be repeated. These costs multiply when a pipeline contains dozens of LLM calls — common in agentic systems.

Automatic prompt optimization addresses all three problems at once. By running optimization against a labeled training set, it evaluates candidate prompts on the actual input distribution rather than a few cherry-picked examples. By treating the prompt as a variable that can be re-optimized programmatically, it makes model upgrades cheap: recompile, measure, deploy. By encoding the task specification as a typed Signature rather than prose, it makes the prompt intent machine-readable and composable across pipeline steps.

For agentic systems specifically, the impact is significant. An agent that routes queries, calls tools, synthesizes results, and generates follow-up questions is really a pipeline of LLM calls. Each call has its own prompt, and optimizing each one independently — while respecting the end-to-end metric — is impractical to do by hand. DSPy makes this tractable: define the pipeline as a DSPy program, supply a small training set, specify an end-to-end metric (such as task success rate), and let the optimizer find the combination of per-module prompts that maximizes it.
: prompts are selected by maximizing a measurable metric on real training data, not by subjective human judgment, leading to reliably better performance.
: the same program can be recompiled for a different model (e.g., switching from GPT-4 to Claude or a local Mistral) without rewriting any prompt text.
: developers write Signatures and pipeline logic once; the optimizer handles prompt wording, saving hours or days of trial-and-error per module.
: multi-step programs (retrieval, classification, generation, verification) are optimized end-to-end rather than one module at a time in isolation, capturing inter-module dependencies.
: the optimization process is deterministic given the same training set and random seed, making results auditable and re-runnable.
: DSPy with a small training set and a good metric often matches or exceeds fine-tuned models on held-out tasks at a fraction of the compute and data cost.

: optimization makes many LLM calls (hundreds to thousands) against the training set. BootstrapFewShot on 50 examples might cost 5–20x the price of a single forward pass; MIPROv2 with a large search budget can cost significantly more.
: even the cheapest optimizers need 20–50 labeled examples with gold answers to bootstrap demonstrations. High-quality labeled data is often the real bottleneck.

: the optimizer can only find prompts that improve the metric it is given. A poorly designed metric (e.g., exact-match on a generation task) will produce worse results than a carefully designed one (e.g., LLM-as-judge with rubric).
: the optimized prompt instructions are machine-generated and can be verbose or unintuitive, making it harder to manually inspect or audit what the model is being asked to do.

: if the training set is too small or not representative, the optimizer can select demonstrations that work well on training examples but poorly on the real distribution. A held-out validation set is essential.

: DSPy programs must be structured using DSPy's Signature and Module abstractions. Migrating an existing prompt-based system requires rewriting it to use DSPy patterns, which can be a significant effort.
DSPy models a program as a computation graph where each node is a
— a callable that wraps an LLM interaction. The most fundamental module is
, which takes a Signature and calls the LLM, returning a structured output. Higher-level modules like
extend Predict by automatically adding a reasoning field, and

specifies the input and output fields with their types and optional descriptions. At compile time, DSPy reads the Signature and constructs a concrete prompt by assembling a header (the task instruction), a block of few-shot demonstrations (if any), and a formatted input. The optimizer's job is to choose the best instruction text and the best set of demonstrations.
optimizer (the simplest) works as follows: for each training example, it runs the uncompiled program to generate a trace of intermediate outputs (the
). It then filters these traces by the metric, keeping only those where the final output was correct. The accepted traces become candidate few-shot demonstrations. Finally, it packs the best
demonstrations into each module's prompt and evaluates the resulting program on a held-out validation set.

optimizer goes further. It uses a Bayesian hyperparameter search (Optuna by default) over two axes simultaneously: the instruction text and the demonstration selection. In each trial, it proposes a new instruction (generated by a meta-LLM that is given the program's Signature and a batch of training examples to learn from), selects a subset of bootstrapped demonstrations, assembles the full prompt, evaluates on a mini-batch of training examples, and reports a score back to the Bayesian optimizer. After the budget is exhausted, the best-scoring configuration is returned as the compiled program.
The optimization objective can be expressed compactly. Given a program

is a scalar score (e.g., exact match, F1, or an LLM-as-judge score). Because the LLM call is non-differentiable, this is a black-box optimization problem — which is why DSPy uses combinatorial search (greedy selection, random search, Bayesian optimization) rather than gradient descent.

DSPy's architecture cleanly separates compile time from inference time. During compilation, an optimizer iterates over training examples, generates candidate prompts via a meta-LLM or bootstrap traces, and evaluates each candidate with the metric function. The winning configuration is stored in the compiled program object and can be saved to disk. At inference time, the compiled program runs as a straightforward Python callable — the prompts are static strings embedded in each module, and no optimizer logic is involved.

The diagram above illustrates the two phases. The compile-time loop (top) passes training examples through the DSPy program, evaluates outputs against the metric, and feeds the score back to the optimizer to guide the next round of prompt proposals. The inference-time pipeline (bottom) executes the compiled program's module chain — Predict, ChainOfThought, ReAct, or custom modules — against the LLM backend, which can be any provider configured via
: declares input/output field names, types, and optional descriptions; drives prompt assembly.
: wraps a single LLM call; holds the current prompt parameters and exposes a
: Python class that chains modules in its
: searches the joint space of instructions and demonstrations; exposes
: any Python callable that takes (prediction, label) and returns a float in [0, 1]; can itself call an LLM for reference-free evaluation.
: the language model API (OpenAI, Anthropic, Cohere, Ollama, HuggingFace, etc.) wired in via

DSPy-style automatic prompt optimization is most valuable when at least one of the following conditions holds: (1) the task has a measurable objective function that can be evaluated programmatically or via LLM-as-judge; (2) the pipeline contains multiple chained LLM calls where manual tuning each one individually would be expensive; (3) the prompt needs to remain performant across model upgrades; or (4) a labeled training set with 50–200 examples is available or can be constructed cheaply.
Question answering and reading comprehension pipelines (clear factual metric)
Multi-hop reasoning chains where each step contributes to a final answer
Classification, labeling, or extraction tasks with structured output and measurable accuracy
Agentic tool-use pipelines where success can be measured as task completion rate
Production systems that require periodic re-optimization as data distribution drifts
Purely creative or open-ended generation tasks with no quantifiable success criterion
One-off or prototype prompts where the compile-time LLM cost exceeds the value of optimization
Applications with strict interpretability requirements where machine-generated prompt text is unacceptable
Systems with no labeled data and no way to construct a proxy metric

The implementation workflow has five steps: install DSPy and configure an LLM backend, define Signatures for each module, compose modules into a DSPy program, define a metric function, and run an optimizer. The result is a compiled program that can be saved with
and loaded at inference time with

pip install dspy
# optional: for MIPROv2 Bayesian search
pip install optuna

"""Step-by-step DSPy setup: configure → define → compose → metric → compile."""
import dspy

# ── 1. Configure the LM backend ──────────────────────────────────────────────
lm = dspy.LM("openai/gpt-4o-mini", api_key="sk-...")
dspy.configure(lm=lm)


# ── 2. Define Signatures ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
class QuestionAnswering(dspy.Signature):
    """Answer a factual question based on the provided context."""
    context: str = dspy.InputField(desc="relevant background text")
    question: str = dspy.InputField(desc="question to answer")
    answer: str = dspy.OutputField(desc="concise factual answer")


# ── 3. Compose a Program ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
class RAGQAProgram(dspy.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        # ChainOfThought wraps the Signature and adds a reasoning field
        self.generate_answer = dspy.ChainOfThought(QuestionAnswering)

    def forward(self, context: str, question: str) -> dspy.Prediction:
        return self.generate_answer(context=context, question=question)


# ── 4. Define a Metric ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
def answer_exact_match(example, prediction, trace=None):
    """Returns 1.0 if the predicted answer contains the gold answer (case-insensitive)."""
    gold = example.answer.strip().lower()
    pred = prediction.answer.strip().lower()
    return 1.0 if gold in pred else 0.0


# ── 5. Prepare a small training set ──────────────────────────────────────────
trainset = [
    dspy.Example(
        context="The Eiffel Tower was built between 1887 and 1889 as the entrance arch for the 1889 World's Fair.",
        question="When was the Eiffel Tower built?",
        answer="1887 to 1889",
    ).with_inputs("context", "question"),
    dspy.Example(
        context="The Python programming language was created by Guido van Rossum and released in 1991.",
        question="Who created Python?",
        answer="Guido van Rossum",
    ).with_inputs("context", "question"),
    # ... add 30–100 more for production-quality optimization
]

# ── 6. Compile with BootstrapFewShot ─────────────────────────────────────────
from dspy.teleprompt import BootstrapFewShot

optimizer = BootstrapFewShot(
    metric=answer_exact_match,
    max_bootstrapped_demos=4,   # max few-shot demos to add per module
    max_labeled_demos=2,        # max hand-labeled demos from trainset
)

program = RAGQAProgram()
compiled_program = optimizer.compile(program, trainset=trainset)

# ── 7. Inspect and save ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
print("Optimized program prompt preview:")
for mod_name, mod in compiled_program.named_predictors():
    print(f"  {mod_name}: {len(mod.demos)} demos, instruction: {mod.extended_signature.instructions[:80]}...")

compiled_program.save("compiled_ragqa.json")

# ── 8. Inference (load and run) ───────────────────────────────────────────────
loaded = RAGQAProgram()
loaded.load("compiled_ragqa.json")

result = loaded(
    context="The Amazon River flows through South America and is the largest river by discharge volume.",
    question="Where does the Amazon River flow?",
)
print("Answer:", result.answer)

A multi-hop QA program chains two modules: one that generates a search query from the question, and one that answers the question given retrieved context. DSPy optimizes both prompts jointly to maximize end-to-end answer accuracy.

import dspy

# Signatures
class GenerateSearchQuery(dspy.Signature):
    """Generate a precise web search query to retrieve facts needed to answer the question."""
    question: str = dspy.InputField()
    query: str = dspy.OutputField(desc="short, specific search query")

class SynthesizeAnswer(dspy.Signature):
    """Given retrieved passages, synthesize a concise factual answer."""
    question: str = dspy.InputField()
    passages: list[str] = dspy.InputField(desc="retrieved text passages")
    answer: str = dspy.OutputField()

# Mock retrieval (replace with a real retriever in production)
def fake_retrieve(query: str, k: int = 3) -> list[str]:
    return [f"Retrieved passage {i} for: {query}" for i in range(k)]

# Multi-hop program
class MultiHopQA(dspy.Module):
    def __init__(self, num_hops: int = 2):
        self.generate_query = [dspy.Predict(GenerateSearchQuery) for _ in range(num_hops)]
        self.synthesize = dspy.ChainOfThought(SynthesizeAnswer)

    def forward(self, question: str):
        passages = []
        for hop_predict in self.generate_query:
            q_result = hop_predict(question=question)
            passages += fake_retrieve(q_result.query)
        return self.synthesize(question=question, passages=passages)

# Run uncompiled
lm = dspy.LM("openai/gpt-4o-mini", api_key="sk-...")
dspy.configure(lm=lm)
multi_hop = MultiHopQA(num_hops=2)
pred = multi_hop(question="What country invented the printing press and in what century?")
print(pred.answer)
Advanced: MIPROv2 for Classification with LLM-as-Judge Metric

MIPROv2 searches both instruction text and demonstration selection simultaneously using Bayesian optimization. The following example uses an LLM-as-judge metric to evaluate free-form sentiment classification, where exact-match would be too strict.

import dspy
from dspy.teleprompt import MIPROv2

lm = dspy.LM("openai/gpt-4o", api_key="sk-...")
dspy.configure(lm=lm)


class SentimentClassifier(dspy.Signature):
    """Classify the sentiment of a customer review as positive, negative, or neutral."""
    review: str = dspy.InputField(desc="customer review text")
    sentiment: str = dspy.OutputField(desc="one of: positive, negative, neutral")
    confidence: str = dspy.OutputField(desc="brief justification for the classification")


class SentimentProgram(dspy.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        self.classify = dspy.ChainOfThought(SentimentClassifier)

    def forward(self, review: str):
        return self.classify(review=review)


def llm_judge_metric(example, prediction, trace=None):
    """Use an LLM to grade the prediction against the gold label."""
    judge = dspy.Predict("gold_label: str, predicted_label: str -> score: float")
    result = judge(
        gold_label=example.sentiment,
        predicted_label=prediction.sentiment,
    )
    try:
        return float(result.score)
    except (ValueError, TypeError):
        return 0.0 if example.sentiment.lower() not in prediction.sentiment.lower() else 1.0


trainset = [
    dspy.Example(review="Absolutely loved the product! Fast shipping too.", sentiment="positive").with_inputs("review"),
    dspy.Example(review="Terrible quality, broke after one day.", sentiment="negative").with_inputs("review"),
    dspy.Example(review="It's okay, does the job but nothing special.", sentiment="neutral").with_inputs("review"),
    # Add more examples for real optimization
]

optimizer = MIPROv2(
    metric=llm_judge_metric,
    auto="light",          # 'light' / 'medium' / 'heavy' — controls search budget
    num_threads=4,         # parallel evaluation threads
)

program = SentimentProgram()
compiled = optimizer.compile(
    program,
    trainset=trainset,
    requires_permission_to_run=False,  # suppress interactive prompt in CI
)

# Evaluate on held-out examples
from dspy.evaluate import Evaluate
devset = [
    dspy.Example(review="Best purchase this year!", sentiment="positive").with_inputs("review"),
    dspy.Example(review="Would not recommend. Poor customer service.", sentiment="negative").with_inputs("review"),
]
evaluate = Evaluate(devset=devset, metric=llm_judge_metric, num_threads=2)
score = evaluate(compiled)
print(f"Dev score: {score:.1%}")

A production customer-support triage system routes incoming tickets to one of five teams (billing, technical, returns, general, escalation) and generates a brief summary for the assigned agent. The pipeline has two stages: a classifier that assigns the routing label, and a summarizer that produces the agent brief. Manual prompt engineering had tuned each stage independently, but end-to-end accuracy (routing label correct
summary rated ≥4/5 by quality reviewers) plateaued at 74%.

After rewriting the pipeline as a DSPy program and running MIPROv2 with 200 labeled tickets and a composite metric (routing accuracy + LLM-judged summary quality), end-to-end accuracy rose to 87% with no human prompt editing. Crucially, when the company migrated from GPT-4 to Claude 3.5, recompiling the same program took four hours of compute and recovered the same 87% baseline, versus the estimated three weeks of manual re-tuning the previous approach would have required.

The key architectural choices in this production deployment were: (1) storing the compiled program JSON in version control alongside the application code, so every model deployment was traceable; (2) running nightly re-optimization on the previous day's tickets labeled by human reviewers, so the prompts tracked distribution drift; and (3) using separate training and validation splits with the same temporal distribution as production to prevent leakage.
Using an exact-match metric on a generation task.

If the expected output is a free-form sentence, exact match will assign zero to many semantically correct predictions, steering the optimizer toward verbose verbatim answers rather than good paraphrases. Use token-level F1, ROUGE, or LLM-as-judge instead.
Compiling on a training set that is not representative of production inputs.
Demonstrations selected from a narrow distribution are poor few-shot examples for out-of-distribution inputs. Always sample training examples that mirror the real query distribution.
Evaluating the compiled program on the training set.
Because the optimizer selected demonstrations from the training set, evaluating on the same set measures overfitting, not generalization. Always use a held-out dev or test set.
It is impossible to know how much the optimizer helped without measuring the uncompiled program's performance on the same dev set first. Always record the baseline before running compilation.
Packing too many demonstrations into each prompt inflates token cost and can exceed the model's context window. Start with 3–5 demos and increase only if the metric improves on the dev set.
Calling program.save() before validating on a dev set.
The optimizer may have overfit or selected degenerate demos that score well on training but fail silently on real inputs. Always evaluate before saving the compiled artifact.
Write descriptive field descriptions in Signatures.
The instruction generator uses Signature descriptions as input when proposing new instructions. The richer the descriptions, the better the proposed instructions.
BootstrapFewShot is cheap and often recovers 70–80% of the gains MIPROv2 achieves. Use it to validate that the setup works before spending the higher budget.
Use a validation split of at least 30–50 examples.
MIPROv2 evaluates candidate prompts on a mini-batch during search; a larger validation set gives more reliable signal and reduces variance in the selected configuration.
Treat the compiled artifact the same as model weights — it encodes a tuned configuration that should be traceable, reviewable, and rollback-able.
Both BootstrapFewShot and MIPROv2 support parallel evaluation threads (
). Enabling this reduces wall-clock compile time by up to the number of threads available.
Design the metric to match the real success criterion.

An LLM-as-judge metric that models what a human reviewer would grade is almost always better than a surface-form metric for generation tasks. Invest time in the metric before running expensive optimization.

Before deploying, read through the few-shot examples the optimizer selected. Occasionally a bootstrapped trace is technically correct but includes reasoning artifacts that could confuse the model on real inputs.

Compile-time cost scales with: the number of training examples, the number of optimizer trials (for MIPROv2), the number of modules in the program, and the token cost per LLM call. A rough estimate: BootstrapFewShot on 50 examples with a 3-module program and a
-token average prompt will consume on the order of
tokens during bootstrapping, plus evaluation passes. MIPROv2 with 40 trials and mini-batch size 20 adds
more tokens. At GPT-4o-mini pricing (~$0.15/M input tokens) this is a few dollars for a simple pipeline and tens of dollars for a complex one — manageable for a one-time compilation.

Inference-time performance is identical to any prompt-based pipeline — the compiled prompts add only a small token overhead compared to a minimal un-demonstrated prompt. The main scalability concern at inference time is the token count of the few-shot demonstrations embedded in each module prompt: 4 demonstrations of 300 tokens each adds 1,200 tokens per module call, which compounds across pipeline steps.
Strategies to reduce inference-time overhead: use
or lower, compress demonstrations by summarizing rather than verbatim inclusion, or use the COPRO optimizer which optimizes instruction text only (no demonstrations), keeping the prompt compact.
): the core framework; includes BootstrapFewShot, BootstrapFewShotWithRandomSearch, MIPROv2, COPRO, KNNFewShot, and LabeledFewShot optimizers.
: Bayesian hyperparameter search library used by MIPROv2 internally (
: a unified gateway for 100+ LLM providers; DSPy supports it natively via
: DSPy emits traces that MLflow can capture, enabling experiment tracking of compile runs, prompt configurations, and metric curves.
: an alternative framework that uses LLM-generated text gradients to optimize prompts via backpropagation-like updates; conceptually similar to COPRO but with different mechanics.
: a prompt testing and evaluation CLI that supports A/B testing of prompt variants and can be used to benchmark compiled vs. uncompiled programs.
: vector databases frequently paired with DSPy retriever modules (
) to implement RAG pipelines whose prompts are then optimized end-to-end.

DSPy occupies a specific niche in the prompt-engineering landscape — automatic, training-data-driven, program-level optimization — that distinguishes it from related approaches. The table below compares the main alternatives on the dimensions that matter most for selection.

Automatically generated prompts introduce several risks that hand-authored prompts do not. Because the optimizer searches a large space of prompt text, it may occasionally discover instructions that produce outputs the developer did not anticipate — including jailbreak-adjacent phrasing that inadvertently weakens content safety constraints. The mitigation is to include adversarial or edge-case examples in the training set and to run the compiled prompt through the same red-teaming or policy-filtering pipeline used for hand-authored prompts before deployment.

The bootstrapped demonstrations embedded in the prompt may contain personally identifiable information (PII) from the training set if that data was not scrubbed. This is particularly concerning when training on production logs. Always anonymize training data before using it for bootstrapping, and add a review step to inspect selected demonstrations for sensitive content before saving the compiled program.

Reliability at inference time is governed by prompt stability. A compiled program's prompts are static strings — they do not mutate at runtime — which makes them easier to cache, version, and audit than prompts assembled dynamically. However, if the compiled program's demonstrations contain model-specific artifacts (e.g., references to specific capability-related behaviors of the model it was compiled on), performance can degrade unexpectedly after a model upgrade. Always re-validate compiled programs after any LLM provider or model-version change.
: read through bootstrapped few-shot examples for unintended content, PII, or policy violations.
: store the compiled JSON in version control and diff it against the previous version before each release.
: do not assume compiled prompts transfer across model versions; always re-run on a held-out eval set to detect regressions.
to avoid hitting API rate limits or incurring unexpected costs during long compilation runs.

. In traditional software, source code (your intent) is compiled into machine code (what actually runs). In DSPy, a program with typed Signatures (your intent) is compiled into a prompt-bearing program (what actually runs). Just as a C compiler knows how to map high-level constructs to efficient machine instructions better than most humans can do by hand, the DSPy optimizer knows how to map a typed task specification to effective prompt text better than most prompt engineers can do by trial-and-error.

The compile step is the expensive investment made once; inference is cheap and fast, exactly like the software analogy. And just as recompiling for a new target architecture (ARM vs. x86) produces different binary code that runs correctly on that target, recompiling a DSPy program for a new model produces different prompt text that is tuned for that model's response patterns.
A second useful mental model: DSPy optimizers perform

. The space of all possible prompts is enormous — every phrasing of every instruction, every possible set of few-shot examples. Human engineers explore a tiny region of this space guided by intuition. The optimizer explores a larger region guided by the metric, using techniques from hyperparameter optimization (random search, Bayesian optimization) that have proven effective in other high-dimensional black-box search problems.

DSPy treats prompt text as an optimizable variable and uses a compiler (the optimizer) to search for the best instructions and few-shot demonstrations, replacing manual prompt engineering with an algorithmic process driven by a training set and a metric function.

The central abstractions are Signatures (typed task declarations), Modules (LLM call wrappers), Programs (composable pipelines), Optimizers, and Metrics — each independently replaceable, enabling model portability with minimal effort.

BootstrapFewShot is the cheapest optimizer (greedy trace selection); MIPROv2 is the most powerful (Bayesian search over instructions and demos) but more expensive; COPRO optimizes instructions only, keeping prompts compact.

Metric quality is the primary lever for optimization quality: a well-designed metric (LLM-as-judge with rubric, task-specific F1) consistently produces better compiled programs than a naive exact-match metric.

Always evaluate the compiled program on a held-out validation set before deployment — overfitting to the training distribution is the most common failure mode, and it is invisible without a clean eval set.
Compile-time cost (hundreds to thousands of LLM calls) is amortized over inference; the compiled program runs at the same speed and token cost as any statically-prompted LLM call.
Treat the compiled program JSON as an artifact to be versioned, audited, and re-validated after every model upgrade — the same discipline applied to model weights in a fine-tuning workflow.
